model.label: Qwen 3.5 Plus
model.description: Alibaba • A balance between effectiveness, speed, and cost.

pick.rationale: Duke remains the clear favorite despite losing starting point guard Caleb Foster to a fractured foot, as the Blue Devils already proved they can win at an elite level without him by capturing the ACC Tournament championship. The efficiency gap is substantial, with Duke's #1 overall ranking and +39.17 adjusted efficiency margin exceeding St. John's +24.8 by roughly 14 points, driven by the nation's best defense and a top-5 offense. While St. John's boasts an elite #12 defense and Rick Pitino's tournament experience, their #46-47 ranked offense lacks the firepower to consistently overcome Duke's defensive dominance, and star guard Kadary Richmond is playing through multiple injuries. Duke's depth, talent level, and proven ability to win without key injured players give them the decisive edge in this Sweet 16 matchup.

reasoningStep.title: Duke vs St. John's Sweet 16 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Duke favored despite injuries due to massive efficiency gap and proven ability to win without Foster; St. John's dangerous but offensive limitations and Richmond's injuries create vulnerability.
reasoningStep.evidence: Duke ranks #1 nationally (KenPom/Torvik) with +39.17 adj EM, #1 defense, #4 offense vs St. John's #18-20 ranking with +24.8 adj EM, #12 defense, #46-47 offense. Duke won ACC Tournament without Foster (fractured foot) and Ngongba II, proving depth. St. John's beat Kansas in R32 but Kadary Richmond playing through multiple injuries. Maliq Brown possibly returning for Duke. 14-point efficiency margin gap favors Duke significantly.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: 2026 March Madness Bracket Predictions – Nate Silver Subscribe Sign in Models & Forecasts 2026 March Madness Predictions Silver Bulletin odds for the men's NCAA tournament, powered by our new COOPER ratings. Nate Silver , Joseph George , and Eli McKown-Dawson Mar 18, 2026 ∙ Paid 108 28 14 Share See also: Women’s March Madness Predictions , Men’s COOPER ratings . 🏀 The latest March Madness projections Updated March 18, 2026 We’ve updated our projections with the results of tonight’s First Four games in Dayton. But almost every office pool starts on Thursday, once the field is reduced to 64 teams, so you still have plenty of time to fill out your picks. We also added new injury data earlier tonight. There were no major changes, but we now have a more pessimistic timeline for any return of Duke’s Caleb Foster. We also toned down the effect of injuries in an update last night. 1 We’ve set up the spreadsheet to include an archive of all previous predictions so you can see the effect of these changes. We’ll continue to update the projections as games are completed . -NS , 3/18/26 Sure, it might seem like the world’s gone a little crazy. But at least we’ll be getting a dose of March Madness. The NCAA tournament is perhaps our very favorite event here at Silver Bulletin. 2 In fact, the tournament really needs no introduction. I’ve been running versions of these projections since 2011, almost ten years before I’d even heard of Substack. But pretty much every part of the model has changed at some point . Instead of compiling other people’s projections, we now mostly rely on our own in the form of our new COOPER ratings — although we blend them with Ken Pomeroy’s ratings (because we think Pomeroy is pretty much the best, present company excluded.) We’ve also upgraded our technology stack. Believe it or not, until last year, part of the work for our NCAA model was still being done in Microsoft Excel. 3 Now we’ve built the code to run proper simulations — 100,000 at a time — which enables more sophisticated handling of injuries (and there are a lot of them this year), among other things. This isn’t an “AI-driven” model quite yet, though Claude and other AI tools are increasingly helping us smooth out some of the code's rough patches and turn these numbers around more quickly. Still, March Madness is eternal. If nothing else, there will be upsets: we’ve calculated that the odds of having a perfect bracket are on the order of 1 in 10 quintillion. I guess I’ll just have to concede that we’re living in a simulation if it happens. How our NCAA forecasts work This is what we call a landing page, meaning that both the numbers and the text will change. We strongly recommend checking out the web version with this link , which provides for better rendering of charts than the Substack app or email. We’ll update these numbers once per day after tournament games are played. In the interest of underpromising and hopefully overdelivering, sometimes the update will need to

You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.

pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.

modelTrace.content:
This is very important information:
1. Duke won the ACC Tournament WITHOUT Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II - this shows they can win without their starting point guard and a starting big man.
2. Foster is out for the "foreseeable future" with a fractured right foot - he's definitely out for the tournament.
3. Maliq Brown (shoulder) could possibly play - so Duke might get one key player back.
4. Duke is still ranked #1 nationally despite these injuries, which speaks to their depth and talent level.

St. John's:
- Ranked 18-20th nationally
- Kadary Richmond is playing through multiple injuries
- Zuby Ejiofor and Joel Soriano appear healthy
- They beat Kansas (a 4-seed ranked 21-22nd) in the R32

The efficiency gap is still substantial:
- Duke: +39.17 adj EM, #1 defense, #4 offense
- St. John's: +24.8 adj EM, #12 defense, #46-47 offense

That's roughly a 14-point adjusted efficiency margin gap favoring Duke.

Duke has already proven they can win without Foster (they won the ACC Tournament without him). St. John's has momentum from beating Kansas, but Duke's overall talent level and defensive dominance should still prevail.

However, the injuries do create some upset potential. Let me finalize my pick with moderate-high confidence given Duke's proven ability to win without Foster, but acknowledging St. John's is a dangerous opponent.
